Learning Objectives

Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to:

  • 01Define spatial analysis and explain how it is used to identify patterns and relationships in geographic data.
  • 02Describe the main types of spatial analysis operations (local, neighborhood, and global) and how they use spatial data.
  • 03Interpret statistical measures used in spatial analysis, including attribute correlation (Pearson’s r and R²).
  • 04Explain spatial autocorrelation and clustering, including the use of Global Moran’s I and Local Moran’s I to detect spatial patterns.

What is spatial analysis? John Snow and the Broad Street Pump

In 1854, a cholera outbreak swept the Soho district of London. Physician John Snow mapped where the deaths occurred and saw them cluster tightly around a single public water pump on Broad Street. He persuaded officials to remove the pump handle, and the outbreak subsided — an early, famous demonstration that where things happen can reveal why they happen.

Explore the John Snow StoryMap →

Defining spatial analysis

Spatial analysis is the application of methods or techniques to examine the spatial (location) and/or non-spatial (attribute) properties of geographic data. It allows researchers to look for explanations of observed patterns: moving from “where is what?” to “why?”, “when?”, “how?”.

Analytical frameworks & operations

Spatial analysis involves inputs and outputs. For example, creating a heat map of the cholera deaths (output) requires a point dataset capturing cholera locations as input. Most spatial analysis involves selecting a function and setting parameters in order to obtain the output.

John Snow did not formally use kernel density analysis to identify the Broad Street Pump. He used the simplest form of spatial analysis: human spatial reasoning.

In spatial analysis, functions/operations are often sequential, forming a “workflow”.

Three types of operations

There are three types of operations:

  • LocalLocal operations: use only data at one input location to determine the value at the corresponding output location.
  • NbhdNeighborhood operations: use data from an input location plus its nearby locations to determine an output value.
  • GlobalGlobal operations: use data values from the entire input layer to determine each output value.

Descriptive measures

We can describe individual features in a spatial dataset by their geometric properties (geometry = geometric).

Feature typeGeometric propertyDescription
Polygon featuresAreaThe extent or measurement of the surface of a 2-D feature.
PerimeterThe boundary measurement of a 2-D feature.
LocationSeries of (x, y); (lat, long).
Line featuresLengthThe measurement of a 1-D linear feature (line).
LocationSeries of (x, y); (lat, long).
Point featuresLocation(x, y); (lat, long).

Describing attributes with statistics

We can describe the non-spatial attributes of spatial data using statistics.

Statistical propertyDescription
MeanAverage value in a dataset.
MedianThe middle number.
Standard deviationA measure of the deviation between each value and the mean.

Describing location with spatial statistics

We can also describe the coordinate information of spatial data using spatial statistics. These are the spatial cousins of the ordinary statistics above — instead of summarizing an attribute column, they summarize where the features are.

Attribute correlation

Attribute correlation examines the statistical relationships between one or more attributes.

One way to measure attribute correlation is Pearson’s correlation coefficient (r). Pearson’s r measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two variables. A value close to 1 indicates a strong positive relationship, meaning that as one variable increases, the other also tends to increase. A value close to −1 indicates a strong negative relationship, meaning that as one variable increases, the other tends to decrease. A value near 0 indicates little or no linear relationship between the variables. In other words, the sign tells you the direction and the magnitude tells you the strength — r always falls between −1 and +1.

Another way is to use the coefficient of determination, R². It generates a single value that tells you how well a linear relationship between two variables explains the variation in the data points. In simple linear regression with two variables, R² is the square of Pearson’s r, so it is always between 0 and 1.

Values closer to 0 indicate that the linear relationship explains very little of the variation in the data, while values closer to 1 indicate that the linear relationship explains most of the variation. For example, an R² value of 0.64 means that 64% of the variation in one variable can be explained by its linear relationship with the other variable.

Explanations for high rates of breast cancer mortality can be explored by examining its correlation to other attributes, such as the percentage of obese individuals and access to exercise opportunities.

Spatial autocorrelation

Tobler’s First Law of Geography states: “Everything is related to everything else, but near things are more related than distant things.” This is why we usually expect nearby places to resemble one another — and why measuring how much they do is so useful.

Spatial autocorrelation (SAC) analysis measures the similarities between spatial location and attributes. In other words, it answers the question: “How similar are neighboring features?”

Global Moran’s I

One method for measuring SAC is called Global Moran’s I. It generates a single value to measure the SAC for the entire dataset. If the value is negative, then the features in the data are NOT similar to each other (negative SAC). If the value is positive, then the features in the data ARE similar to each other (positive SAC). A value near 0 suggests the pattern is essentially random — positive values mean clustering, negative values mean a dispersed, checkerboard-like pattern.

Check in
Is Global Moran’s I a local, neighborhood, or global operation? Why?
Answer Global. It produces a single value for the whole dataset, using every feature in the layer at once.

Local Moran’s I

Another method for measuring SAC is called Local Moran’s I. It generates a SAC value for every feature in the dataset that characterizes how similar it is to its neighbors. This allows us to make a map of clusters of similar features and outliers that are different from their neighbors. For example, Local Moran’s I calculates a different value for each county that shows whether its breast cancer mortality rate is similar to or different from nearby counties.

  • H–HHigh–High cluster: high values surrounded by high values.
  • L–LLow–Low cluster: low values surrounded by low values.
  • H–LHigh–Low outlier: a high value surrounded by low values.
  • L–HLow–High outlier: a low value surrounded by high values.
  • n/sNot significant: not a statistically significant cluster or outlier.
Check in
Is Local Moran’s I a local, neighborhood, or global operation? Why?
Answer Neighborhood. Each feature’s value depends on that feature together with its nearby neighbors — not just itself, and not the whole layer.
Check in
What type of SAC is each pattern below — positive, negative, or none? Work it out, then raise your hand.
Check in
Point to a highlighted county on the Local Moran’s I map above — is it H–H, L–L, L–H, or H–L? I’ll call on someone.
Check in
Now use the numbers. For each county below, what type of cluster or outlier is it? Use the sign of Ii, the standardized rate, and the p-value. Work it out, then raise your hand.
How to read it First check the p-value: if p ≥ 0.05 it’s Not significant. Otherwise, the sign of Ii says cluster vs. outlier (positive = similar to neighbors → cluster; negative = different → outlier), and the standardized rate says whether the county’s own value is high (positive) or low (negative).
1
Ii = 1.45, p = 0.04, rate = −1.9 → significant, positive Ii (cluster), low value → Low–Low cluster.
2
Ii = 0.20, p = 0.55 → Not significant (p ≥ 0.05).
3
Ii = 1.82, p = 0.01, rate = 2.1 → significant, positive Ii (cluster), high value → High–High cluster.
4
Ii = −1.69, p = 0.03, rate = 3.4 → significant, negative Ii (outlier), high value among low → High–Low outlier.
5
Ii = 1.01, p = 0.06 → Not significant (p ≥ 0.05).
6
Ii = −1.35, p = 0.00, rate = −1.7 → significant, negative Ii (outlier), low value among high → Low–High outlier.

The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem

Statistics are dependent on scale.

Statistics are dependent on zone.

A real-world example from Vancouver, Canada:

The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP): the same basic data yields different results when aggregated in different ways. Decisions on how spatial data is aggregated are determined by the type of phenomena and the biases of the investigator. Thus, the areal units used in many geographic studies are arbitrary and modifiable.

The practical caution: be skeptical when comparing statistics computed at different scales or zonings — a correlation can strengthen, vanish, or even flip sign purely because of how the areas were drawn.

Key takeaways

1
Spatial analysis examines spatial and attribute data to move from describing patterns to explaining processes.
2
Spatial analysis workflows combine inputs, functions, and outputs, often applying operations sequentially to transform geographic data.
3
Spatial data can be analyzed two ways: the analysis of spatial data (attribute statistics and correlation) and spatial data analysis (spatial statistics that incorporate location — mean center, median center, spatial autocorrelation).
4
Spatial results depend on how data are aggregated — the MAUP can influence statistical outcomes.

Key terms & concepts

Open any term to see its definition and an example.
